key_questions: Triggers: food, medication, infection, exercise, stress, cold, pressure?
New medications in past 6 weeks (especially NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, antibiotics)?
red_flags: Angioedema of tongue/throat or stridor → anaphylaxis/airway emergency — CALL 911
Systemic symptoms (fever, arthralgia, lymphadenopathy) → vasculitis, autoinflammatory
Chronic urticaria >6 weeks → requires investigation, refer to allergist

first_line.name: Non-Sedating Oral Antihistamine
first_line.drugs: Cetirizine (Reactine) 10 mg OD–BID | Fexofenadine (Allegra) 180 mg OD | Loratadine (Claritin) 10 mg OD
first_line.notes: Non-sedating H1 antihistamines: first-line for acute urticaria. Can be used up to 4× standard dose in refractory urticaria. Take regularly (not PRN) for better effect. Cetirizine 10 mg OD–BID: most commonly used. Continue until urticaria fully resolved.

second_line.name: Sedating Antihistamine (short-term add-on)
second_line.drugs: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 25–50 mg q6h (avoid in elderly — Beers Criteria)
second_line.notes: For severe acute urticaria — short-term use. High sedation and anticholinergic burden. Avoid in elderly (falls, confusion). Not for chronic urticaria.

second_line.name: Short-Course Prednisone
second_line.notes: For severe acute urticaria not responding to antihistamines. Do not use for chronic urticaria (no long-term benefit; significant side effects).

non_pharm: Identify and avoid triggers (food diary, medication review)
treatment.refer_when: Chronic urticaria >6 weeks; angioedema; suspected hereditary angioedema (no wheals, family history); urticaria + systemic symptoms; failure to respond to antihistamines
